components.role: First gain stage — germanium PNP provides initial gain with soft, warm clipping characteristics
Second gain stage — driven into saturation for main fuzz tone, germanium provides soft clipping knee
Q1 collector resistor — note: PNP topology uses negative supply convention
Q1 emitter resistor — thermal stability for temperature-sensitive germanium
Q1 base bias — high value for bias stability with leaky germanium
Q2 base bias — coupled from Q1 collector
Fuzz control — adjusts feedback between Q2 collector and Q1 base
Bias trim resistor — compensates for germanium transistor leakage variation
Input coupling — larger value than silicon fuzz for more bass content

dsp_model.description: High gain cascaded stages with softer clipping knee than silicon; includes sag/gating behavior from bias sensitivity

behavioral_description: The classic vintage germanium fuzz sound — warm, woolly, sputtery, and deeply musical. Germanium PNP transistors (like the AC128) have lower gain and a softer clipping characteristic than silicon, producing a smoother, more rounded distortion with less harsh upper harmonics. The sound is thick and dark with a characteristic sag — as the signal decays, the bias point shifts and the fuzz can gate abruptly, creating that famous 'velcro' dying note texture. Germanium transistors are notoriously temperature-sensitive; the bias point drifts with heat, which some players consider part of the charm (the pedal literally sounds different as it warms up). The PNP topology means the circuit runs on negative ground, which can complicate power supply daisy-chaining. This is the sound of Hendrix's Fuzz Face, the tone that defined psychedelic rock.
